content: A parallel plate capacitor with air between the plates has a capacitance of $$15 \mathrm{~pF}$$. the separation between the plates is $$d$$. The space between the plates is now filled with two dielectrics constant $$k_1=3$$ and thickness $$d / 3$$ while the other one has dielectric constant $$k_2=6$$ and thickness $$2 d / 3$$. Capacitance of the capacitor is now
explanation:
$$\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}=15 \mathrm{pF}$$ (given)

$$C_{\text {eq }}=\frac{C_1 C_2}{C_1+C_2}=\frac{\frac{k_1 \varepsilon_0 A}{d / 3} \times \frac{k_2 \varepsilon_0 A}{2 d / 3}}{\frac{k_1 \varepsilon_0 A}{d / 3}+\frac{k_2 \varepsilon_0 A}{2 d / 3}}$$

$$\begin{aligned} = & \left(\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}\right)\left(\frac{3 k_1 k_2}{2 k_1+k_2}\right)=15 \times \frac{3 \times 3 \times 6}{2 \times 3+6}=\frac{135}{2} \\ \Rightarrow \quad & C_{\text {eq }}=67.5 ~p F \end{aligned}$$

content: A capacitor is filled with two dielectrics of the same dimensions but of dielectric constants 2 and 3 as shown in Fig.(a) and then in Fig.(b). Then ratio of the capacitor in the two arrangements is
explanation:
In Fig. (a), $$C=\frac{2 \varepsilon_0 \frac{A}{2}}{d}+\frac{3 \varepsilon_0 \frac{A}{2}}{d}=\frac{5 \varepsilon_0 A}{2 d}$$

In Fig. (b),

$$\begin{aligned} & C^{\prime}=\frac{\left(\frac{3 \varepsilon_0 A}{\frac{d}{2}}\right)\left(\frac{2 \varepsilon_0 A}{\frac{d}{2}}\right)}{\frac{2 \varepsilon_0 A}{\frac{d}{2}}+\frac{3 \varepsilon_0 A}{\frac{d}{2}}} \\ & C^{\prime}=\frac{6 \varepsilon_0 A}{\frac{d / 2}{5}}=\frac{12 \varepsilon_0 A}{5 d} \\ & \frac{C}{C^{\prime}}=\frac{5 \varepsilon_0 A}{2 d} \times \frac{5 d}{12 \varepsilon_0 A}=\frac{25}{24} \end{aligned}$$

content: A parallel plate capacitor with plate area $$A$$ and separation between the plates $$d$$ is charged by a constant current $$i$$. Consider a plane surface of area $$A / 2$$ parallel to the plates and drawn simultaneously between the plates. The displacement current through this area is
explanation:
Charge on capacitor plates at time $$t$$ is given as, $$q=it$$.

Electric field between the plates at this instant.

$$E=\frac{q}{A \varepsilon_0}=\frac{i t}{A \varepsilon_0}$$

Electric flux through the given area

$$\phi_E=\left(\frac{A}{2}\right) E=\frac{i t}{2 \varepsilon_0}$$

Thereiore, displacement current,

$$i_d=\varepsilon_0 \frac{d \phi_E}{d t}=\varepsilon_0 \frac{d}{d t}\left(\frac{i t}{2 \varepsilon_0}\right)=\frac{i}{2}$$

content: A parallel plate capacitor of capacitance $$5 \mu \mathrm{F}$$ is charged to $$120 \mathrm{~V}$$ and then connected to another uncharged capacitor. If the potential falls to $$40 \mathrm{~V}$$, and capacitance of the second capacitor is
explanation:
Change, $$Q=C V$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Given,} \quad & C=5 \mu \mathrm{F}=5 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~F}, \mathrm{~V}=120 \mathrm{~V} \\ & Q=5 \times 10^{-6} \times 120 \end{aligned}$$

Also, $$5 \times 10^{-6} \times 120=40 \times C$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & C=\frac{5 \times 10^{-6} \times 120}{40}=15 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~F} \\ \Rightarrow & C=15 \mu \mathrm{F} \end{array}$$

content: Two identical capacitors are first connected in series and then in parallel. The ratio of equivalent capacitance is
explanation:
Capacitor in series $$\frac{1}{C_s}=\frac{1}{C}+\frac{1}{C}=\frac{2}{C}$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad C_s=\frac{C}{2}$$

Capacitor in parallel $$C_p=C+C=2 C$$

$$\therefore \quad \frac{C_s}{C_p}=\frac{C}{2 \times 2 C}=\frac{1}{4}$$
